source.title: Onset and offset of aversive events establish distinct memories requiring fear and reward networks

confidence_rationale: Cross-species (human fMRI + rat lesion) double dissociation: relief activates and requires striatum (reward circuit), fear activates and requires amygdala. This is the strongest single piece of evidence in the slate that relief is computed in the reward network, not in a parallel aversive-only circuit. Mapping fidelity is high because the experiment directly tests where the relief signal is computed; transfer risk is low because the human and rat results converge.
failure_signatures: Localises a relief signal to the ventral striatum but does not by itself rule out parallel safety-signal encoding in vmPFC; the IL/PL contribution is not tested here.
Cannot distinguish whether the striatal relief signal uses the same neurons / same DA receptors as reward, only that the same gross structure is required.

mapping.source_claim_statement: A relief-conditioned cue activates ventral striatum (and not amygdala) in human fMRI, and ventral-striatum inactivation in rats abolishes conditioned relief but not conditioned fear. Conversely, a fear-conditioned cue activates amygdala (and not striatum), and amygdala inactivation abolishes fear but not relief.
mapping.ree_translation: The relief-completion pathway shares its substrate with the reward / goal-achievement pathway (ventral striatum / NAc), not with the aversive/threat pathway (amygdala). This is direct biological grounding for Model 1 in REE: a suffering-reduction completion event should be processed by the same downstream machinery as goal-achievement, with the polarity of the reinforcement signal handled at the input rather than via parallel circuitry.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper localises relief to the ventral striatum but does not test infralimbic / vmPFC encoding of safety signals (which other papers in this slate do localise). So Model 1 is supported for the reinforcement / completion event itself, but the predictive encoding of safety cues may still have a parallel substrate.
mapping.source_context: Adult human participants in fMRI + Sprague-Dawley rats with localised muscimol inactivation; Pavlovian relief and fear conditioning paradigms with mild electric shock as the aversive US.

confidence_components.notes: Cross-species double dissociation is the strongest available form of architectural evidence for this question. Reduces transfer risk substantially compared to either species alone.
